planting.note: Sow seeds in spring or direct sow in fall in moist, moderately fertile to rich soil in full sun; giant inula requires substantial space and strong, deep, moist soil to achieve its maximum size and effect. It does not tolerate dry conditions well. A single established plant can spread over 4 feet and may require support in exposed, windy positions.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, fertile soil in full sun; giant inula earns its name — one of the most dramatically large and architecturally bold perennials in temperate gardens, producing enormous basal leaves that can exceed 3 feet in length and coarse, winged, branching stems reaching 6–10 feet in rich, moist conditions. In July and August these giant stems carry loose clusters of golden-yellow, spider-petaled daisy flowers (3–4 inches across, with very fine, thread-like ray petals similar to those of I. hookeri but on a vastly larger scale) that are individually beautiful but produce their greatest impact through the sheer dramatic scale of the plant bearing them. Native to the Caucasus region, it thrives in moist, fertile ground near water features, at the back of large, wide borders, and in any bold, naturalistic planting where its monumental scale is an asset rather than a problem. A single specimen in a choice position in a large garden creates an immediate focal point of extraordinary presence during its late-summer bloom period. Attracts bees and butterflies enthusiastically and provides seeds for birds. Not suitable for small gardens.

planting.note: Sow seeds in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; Russell's sage is drought-tolerant once established. Do not cut the spent flower stems — the dried, brown whorled stem structures are attractive through winter and provide bird food. Divide clumps every 4–5 years in spring to maintain vigor.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; Russell's sage is a Turkish native and one of the most structurally interesting and drought-tolerant perennials for the sunny border — forming bold, spreading mounds of large, heart-shaped, rough-textured, wrinkled, gray-green leaves and in June through July producing upright stems carrying whorled tiers of soft, butter-yellow, hooded, sage-like flowers arranged in distinct rings at regular intervals up the stem, creating an architectural, tiered candelabra form. After the flowers fade, the calyx whorls remain on the dried stems through autumn and winter as attractive, rigid, tiered seed structures in warm brown, providing months of winter architectural interest and seeds for finches. The dried winter stems are so decorative that Chelsea Flower Show designers have used them as a winterbourne architectural element in naturalistic plantings. Outstanding for the gravel garden, prairie-style border, and drought-tolerant plantings combined with ornamental grasses and other drought-adapted plants. Visited heavily by bumblebees.

planting.note: Sow seeds in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; tuberous Jerusalem sage is one of the most cold-hardy phlomis species (to zone 3) due to its tuberous roots that protect it through severe winters. As with P. russelliana, leave the dried whorled stems standing through winter for ornamental interest and wildlife value.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; tuberous Jerusalem sage is one of the most frost-hardy phlomis species — surviving reliably to zone 3 due to its fleshy tuberous roots — and differs from the more common P. russelliana in producing rosy-mauve to lilac-pink whorled flowers (rather than yellow) on taller, more upright stems reaching 4–5 feet. The flower color in shades of warm mauve-pink is unusual in the phlomis genus, which is more commonly represented by yellow species. Like all phlomis, the flowers are arranged in distinct, evenly-spaced whorls along the upright stem, creating the characteristic, architecturally tiered, candelabra effect at bloom time in June and July. The dried stems and calyx whorls persist through winter as attractive structures. More upright and taller than P. russelliana. Native to central and eastern Europe and central Asia in dry, rocky, steppic grassland. Outstanding for prairie-style plantings, gravel gardens, and large-scale naturalistic designs where drought tolerance and winter stem interest are both valued.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring; Salvia x superba is a sterile hybrid and must be propagated vegetatively by division or cuttings. After the first main flush (typically June–July), shear the entire plant back hard by one-half to two-thirds — this will produce a second, often more prolific flush of bloom by August–September and prevent the plant from going to seed before it can rebloom.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; superb sage is one of the foundational hybrid garden salvias — a sterile perennial hybrid related to Salvia nemorosa and S. sylvestris that produces dense, erect spikes of small, rich violet-purple to blue-violet, hooded flowers with contrasting dark, wine-purple calyces, giving the spike a two-toned color effect. The dense spikes appear on bushy, upright plants in June–July and, if the whole plant is cut back hard after the first flowering, again in August–September — one of the most reliable and rewarding reblooming perennials if the shearing step is reliably performed. The cultivar 'Superba' has been in cultivation since the 1930s and spawned a large group of related hybrids now grouped under Salvia nemorosa and S. x sylvestris. Outstanding for the sunny, drought-tolerant border combined with yellow achillea, silver artemisia, and ornamental grasses. The flower spikes retain their decorative character as they age, the dried calyces remaining attractive for weeks after the petals fall. Reliably attractive to bees — particularly bumblebees — throughout the bloom period.

planting.note: Sow seeds in early spring in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full shade; sticky sage is one of very few salvias that genuinely thrives in shade — most salvias require full sun, making this species uniquely valuable for shaded situations. The sticky stems and leaves can be irritating to sensitive skin; handle with care or wear gloves.

directions: Plant in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part to full shade; sticky sage is a woodland native of mountain forests across central and southern Europe and Asia Minor — one of the very few salvias genuinely adapted to shade, making it uniquely valuable as a large, flowering herb for shaded sites where virtually all other sages would fail. The large, hairy, somewhat sticky (glandular) leaves are strongly aromatic when rubbed, with an odor somewhat like oregano mixed with damp vegetation. In summer the tall stems produce whorled clusters of pale lemon-yellow to soft yellow flowers with a darker reddish-brown spot in the throat — smaller than many cultivated salvias but distinctive in their soft yellow color (unusual among salvias, which are most commonly blue, purple, or red). The sticky-hairy texture of the plant is responsible for the common name 'sticky sage' and makes the leaves adhesive to the touch. It self-sows modestly in appropriate shaded conditions to maintain small colonies. A rare example of a shade-adapted Salvia, outstanding combined with hostas, ferns, and woodland wildflowers in moist, cool, semi-shaded positions.
